questions.a: A single deployable application that contains most of the product logic.
A small service focused on one business capability and deployed independently.
How strongly components depend on each other.
How closely related the responsibilities inside a module are.
A boundary where a domain model has a clear meaning.
A set of object-oriented design principles.

questions.q: Why does a modular monolith often age better than a big ball of mud?
questions.a: It keeps boundaries explicit without the overhead of distributed deployment.
It keeps high-level policy from depending directly on low-level details.

questions.q: Why does hexagonal architecture place adapters at the edge?
questions.a: To keep business logic independent of transport and storage concerns.

questions.q: Why do event-driven systems need clear contracts?
questions.a: Events become integration points and must stay stable and understandable.

questions.q: Why does CQRS split reads and writes?
questions.a: Because the read model and write model often have different performance needs.

questions.q: Why is shared database access often a smell in service design?
questions.a: It couples services through schema changes and hidden write paths.

questions.q: When should you split a service boundary?
questions.a: When ownership, scaling, or change cadence clearly diverge.

questions.q: When is a layered architecture enough?
questions.a: When the domain is small and the team benefits from simplicity over abstraction.
Independent deployment and scale, but more operational and distributed complexity.

questions.q: Why should core business rules live near the domain model?
questions.a: So they are easier to test, reuse, and protect from infrastructure churn.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with a clean architecture boundary?
questions.a: You get easier change management, but you may introduce extra indirection.

questions.q: When is a refactor toward better boundaries worth it?
questions.a: When repeated change, bugs, or ownership friction justify the cost.
